text: Two cars. Same model. Same road.
visual: Side view of a highway. Two identical cars sit at the start of the frame, one slightly behind the other.

text: The first one is doing sixty. The second one is doing a hundred and twenty. Twice the speed.
visual: Both cars begin moving. A speedometer overlays each: '60 km/h' above the first, '120 km/h' above the second. The second car pulls ahead noticeably.

text: Common sense: twice the speed, twice the danger. Right?
visual: The two speedometers freeze side by side. A scale icon appears between them, balanced evenly. A small label: '2× speed = 2× danger?'

text: Wrong.
visual: The scale tips violently to one side. The label crosses out.

text: Four times.
visual: The number '4×' fades in large in the center of the frame, then dims.

text: Not a little more. Not a bit more. Four times the energy slamming into whatever you hit.
visual: The two cars from earlier appear again, but this time with energy bars above each. The 60 km/h car's bar is short. The 120 km/h car's bar is four times as tall.

text: That doesn't match common sense. So let's leave cars for a minute. Stand on a wall.
visual: The cars fade out. A simple side-view illustration appears: a person standing on top of a low wall, ground beneath them.

text: The wall is one meter high. Jump down.
visual: A dashed line shows the wall's height labeled '1 m.' The figure hops off and lands on the ground with a small impact ring.

text: Your knees take it. No big deal. You've done it a hundred times.
visual: The figure walks away casually. A green checkmark briefly pulses near them.

text: Now make the wall four meters.
visual: The wall stretches upward dramatically. The dashed height label changes from '1 m' to '4 m.' The figure stands at the top, much higher than before.

text: Stop. Don't jump. Think first.
visual: The figure hesitates at the edge of the 4-meter wall. The frame holds still.

text: Four times higher. So… four times harder to land? That's what common sense says.
visual: Two stick figures appear side by side: one standing under a '1 m' marker, one under a '4 m' marker. A scale shows '4×' between them.

text: But you can feel in your stomach that it's worse than that. Way worse.
visual: A faint red glow pulses around the 4-meter figure. A small thought bubble shows a knee with a sharp pain indicator.

text: Your stomach is right. Jumping from four meters isn't four times worse than from one. It's sixteen times worse.
visual: The '4×' label gets crossed out. A new label fades in larger: '16×.' The red glow around the 4-meter figure intensifies.

text: From one meter — your knees absorb it. From four meters — you break your legs.
visual: Side-by-side outcomes: the 1-meter jumper walks away with a green checkmark. The 4-meter jumper is on the ground with red impact lines around their legs.

text: What happened? You only raised the height by four. The damage went up by sixteen.
visual: An arrow on the left side shows height multiplied by 4. An arrow on the right shows damage multiplied by 16. The two arrows are visibly different sizes.

text: Because falling further means landing faster. And the energy your body has when you land doesn't grow with speed. It grows with speed squared.
visual: A simple graph appears. X-axis labeled 'speed.' Y-axis labeled 'energy.' A straight diagonal line is drawn — then erased. A steep upward curve replaces it.

text: Double your landing speed — four times the impact. Triple it — nine times. The number on top is a power, not a multiplier.
visual: Three columns rise from the floor: speed ×1 → impact ×1, speed ×2 → impact ×4, speed ×3 → impact ×9. Each column is much taller than expected.

text: Physics writes it like this.
visual: The equation fades in elegantly in the center of the frame: KE = ½ m v²

text: Most of the symbols are boring. Mass. A half. The one that matters is the little two on top of the v.
visual: The 'm' and '½' fade to gray. The 'v²' glows. A small arrow points to the '²' exponent.

text: That little two is the reason a four-meter fall is sixteen times worse than a one-meter fall.
visual: Callback split. Left: the 1-meter jumper walking away. Right: the 4-meter jumper on the ground. The '²' from the equation hovers between them, glowing.

text: Now. Back to the cars.
visual: The fall scenes dissolve. The two cars from the opening return, side by side on the road.

text: Sixty to a hundred and twenty isn't doubling the danger. It's the four-meter wall instead of the one-meter wall. Same body. Same physics.
visual: Above each car, a wall fades in: a 1-meter wall above the 60 km/h car, a 4-meter wall above the 120 km/h car. The figure on each wall mirrors the earlier illustration.

text: Hit a wall at sixty — your car crumples. You might walk out.
visual: The 60 km/h car collides with a barrier. The front crumples. A figure steps out of the door.

text: Hit the same wall at a hundred and twenty — four times the energy has to go somewhere. It goes into the car. And what's left of you.
visual: The 120 km/h car collides with the same barrier. The entire car deforms severely. No figure emerges. The frame holds, sober.

text: It's not the speedometer that kills people. It's the little two.
visual: Close-up of a speedometer needle climbing past 120. The '²' from the equation appears as a faint ghost overlay on the dial.

text: Twice as fast is never twice as bad. It's four times. Every time. No exceptions.
visual: A simple final image: two cars side by side at rest. Above the slower one, a small energy bar. Above the faster one, a bar four times as tall. Hold.

text: So next time you press the accelerator — you're not adding speed. You're squaring it.
visual: First-person view from inside a car. A hand on a steering wheel. A foot presses the accelerator pedal. The speedometer climbs. As it climbs, the '²' faintly glows on the dial.
